response:
Read the memo and the usage CSV. Decision recorded in `DECISION.md`:

**Cap: 300 requests/day per key.**

**Why not 1,000:** That number is on the table only because the metering rig already supports it — that's an infrastructure fact, not a reason. At 1,000 the cap would never bind for any observed user (fleet average is ~94–106/key/day across all 30 days), which makes the free tier effectively unmetered and defeats the one thing the memo says the decision rides on: a lever toward Pro conversion.

**Why not ~150 (near observed mean):** The CSV only has fleet-level daily totals, not per-key data. The per-key average is oddly tight day over day (94–106), which is exactly what averaging over ~40 keys would produce even if some individual keys run at 250–400/day — that tail is invisible in this data. Capping near the mean risks throttling ordinary users on a normal heavier day, before Pro has proven anything, for zero offsetting benefit.

**Why 300:** ~3x the fleet-average day (absorbs normal variance), well under a third of the infra ceiling (still a real constraint on actual heavy users — the population that should convert), and low enough to read as a deliberate tier boundary rather than an infra accident.

**What changes it:** lower if per-key p90/p95 data (once logged) shows 300 isn't actually binding on anyone; lower if conversions stay near zero and free users never see the cap; raise if launch produces a wave of complaints from clearly ordinary usage hitting 300; handle isolated legitimate high-volume cases (e.g. CI/OSS) via allowlist rather than moving the global cap.

Flagged as immediate follow-up: add per-key percentile logging now — the current CSV can't distinguish "everyone uses ~100/day" from "most use ~80 and a few use ~400," and that distinction should drive the 60–90 day revisit once Pro has real conversion data.
